turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Kagan has the opinion of the Court this morning in case 21-86, Axon Enterprise versus FTC, and the consolidated case 21-1239, SEC versus Cochran.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: This case is about where, meaning in what court, a person can bring an unusual kind of lawsuit. It's actually two cases, which we decided to hear together, because they raise the same issue. They both involve administrative agencies. In one, Federal Trade Commission charged a company called Axon with violating competition law. In the other, the Securities and Exchange Commission charged an accountant with violating auditing standards. I am just going to refer to both parties as Axon. When it was charged, Axon decided to raise a sweeping challenge to the agency's authority. This challenge had nothing to do with whether Axon actually violated the law, nor did Axon charged that the agency had made any particular mistakes in handling the case. Instead Axon argued that the agency was unconstitutionally structured, so that it simply could not bring these kinds of complaints really against anyone. Axon's main complaint had to do with administrative law judges or ALJs. Those judges are the frontline decision makers in cases like this. An ALJ decides whether a company violated competition law. And to preserve ALJs' independence when they make those decisions, the law makes them hard to fire. But Axon says that the law makes them so hard to fire that it violates the Constitution. The idea is that these ALJs who were in apart of the Executive Branch are too removed from presidential supervision, and that, Axon says, violates the constitutional principle of separation of powers. So the agency cannot bring these cases at all. Now today, we don't say a word about whether that's a good argument. Instead we decide whether Axon can raise it in a federal district court. The reason that's a hard question is that Congress has set out a particular way for people to object to agency proceedings. The relevant statutes say that a person like Axon first has to raise such objections to the agency itself, and if that doesn't work, Axon can take its case to a court of appeals. The district court has no role. Instead the agency fills in for what the district court usually does. But Axon didn't want to have to go through the agency. Axon though it was a bit a meticulous to have to take that route when it was arguing that most of what the agency does is unconstitutional. Axon though it should be able to bring a claim like that in the same way someone brings a normal lawsuit, not with the agency itself, but with the federal district court, and today, we agree with Axon. In prior cases, we have developed a mechanism for deciding whether a party like Axon can bypass the agency and bring complaints about agency proceedings directly in district court. We ask three questions that are usually known as the Thunder Basin factors, sort of a dramatic name, but they're just name for the case where we originally set out those questions. So here are the three questions. Number one, would following the routes though the agencies foreclose meaningful judicial review? Number two, Is Axon's claim wholly collateral to, meaning separate from, what the agency does in its ordinary work? And number three, does the agency have any expertise in handling the kind of claim that Axon is bringing? Here we think all those factors point in the direction of allowing Axon to bring its claim in federal court. So I am going to go backwards. Going backwards, the agency has no particular expertise in asking or answering big separations of powers questions, rather the opposite, and we've said this many times before. Second those big constitutional questions are detached from what the agency would be doing in these proceedings, that is deciding whether Axon violated competition law. And third, forcing Axon to go through the agency would deprive him of meaningful judicial review of his claim. Not because his claim is all about being subjected to illegitimate agency proceedings. So if he had to go through those proceedings to raise his claim, he had suffered the very kind of harm he is complaining about before he gets to any court. The upshot is that Axon can bring its bid constitutional claim in district court. It need not go through the agency when its claim is that the entire agency is unconstitutional. Justice Thomas has filed a concurring opinion. Justice Gorsuch has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment.
